Headline: The Era of Discovery and Chip Evolution
Text: A comprehensive chronological overview of the history of semiconductors, from the first physical observations of the 19th century to the era of Artificial Intelligence and quantum chips.

Headline: Michael Faraday Discovers the First "Clue"
Text: He notes that silver sulfide behaves inversely to normal metals: when heated, its electrical conductivity increases instead of decreasing, showing the typical behavior of a semiconductor.
Group: The Era of Discovery - Origins

Headline: Karl Ferdinand Braun Discovers Asymmetric Conduction
Text: He notices that current passes more easily in one direction than the other when touching a galena crystal with a metallic point. The first rudimentary diode (the "cat's whisker") is born, used for early radios.
Group: The Era of Discovery - Origins

Headline: The Invention of the Transistor
Text: At Bell Labs, John Bardeen, Walter Brattain, and William Shockley create the first point-contact transistor using germanium. This tiny device can amplify signals or act as a switch, replacing thermionic valves.
Group: The Breakthrough: From Transistor to Chip

Headline: Silicon Arrives
Text: Morris Tanenbaum at Bell Labs (and shortly after, Texas Instruments) creates the first working silicon transistor. Silicon withstands much higher temperatures and is incredibly abundant on our planet (a component of sand).
Group: The Breakthrough: From Transistor to Chip

Headline: The First Integrated Circuit (Chip)
Text: Jack Kilby of Texas Instruments conceives the idea of manufacturing multiple components (transistors, resistors, capacitors) all on the same piece of material (germanium at the time) without using connecting wires. The first integrated circuit in history is born.
Group: The Breakthrough: From Transistor to Chip

Headline: The Modern Silicon Chip
Text: Robert Noyce (Fairchild Semiconductor) independently invents an integrated circuit, but using silicon and the planar process, employing microscopic metallic lines printed directly onto the silicon to connect the components.
Group: The Breakthrough: From Transistor to Chip

Headline: The MOSFET Transistor is Invented
Text: Designed by Kahng and Atalla at Bell Labs, it becomes the easiest type of transistor to miniaturize and remains the absolute standard used in modern chips today.
Group: Commercial Explosion and Microprocessors
Source: Computer History Museum — MOS transistor demonstrated

Headline: Gordon Moore Formulates His Famous "Law"
Text: Gordon Moore predicts that the number of transistors on a chip will double every 12 to 24 months, drastically reducing costs and increasing computing power.
Group: Commercial Explosion and Microprocessors

Headline: Robert Noyce and Gordon Moore Found Intel
Text: The multinational corporation destined to become the pillar and global benchmark for microprocessor development and manufacturing is born.
Group: Commercial Explosion and Microprocessors

Headline: The Microprocessor is Born (Intel 4004)
Text: Designed by the Italian Federico Faggin, it integrates the entire CPU onto a single piece of silicon the size of a fingernail, containing 2,300 transistors, overcoming the old division across multiple chips.
Group: Commercial Explosion and Microprocessors

Headline: Intel Launches the 8080
Text: An evolution of the 4004 with an 8-bit bus and around 6,000 transistors. This is the chip that officially kicks off the commercial revolution of early personal computers like the Altair 8800.
Group: The 70s and 80s: The Birth of the Personal Computer
Source: Tom's Hardware Italia — L'Intel 8080 compie 50 anni

Headline: The Low-Cost MOS Technology 6502 Chip is Introduced
Text: Thanks to its incredibly low cost compared to Intel's competition, Steve Jobs and Steve Wozniak choose it for the Apple I and Apple II. It would also become the heart of the Commodore 64 and Nintendo's NES.
Group: The 70s and 80s: The Birth of the Personal Computer

Headline: Intel Releases the 8086 Processor
Text: A 16-bit processor that introduces and establishes the "x86" architectural standard. This is the exact same instruction set that, although evolved, powers most of today's PCs and servers.
Group: The 70s and 80s: The Birth of the Personal Computer

Headline: The Intel 386 Arrives
Text: Introduces advanced 32-bit memory management, enabling true multitasking for complex programs. It is the hardware component that makes Windows and modern operating systems truly usable. It contains 275,000 transistors.
Group: The 70s and 80s: The Birth of the Personal Computer

Headline: The TSMC Revolution
Text: Until this moment, those who designed chips had to own the expensive factories to manufacture them. TSMC introduces the "pure-play foundry" model for third parties, allowing new companies (like NVIDIA) to focus solely on design.
Group: The 90s: The Era of Multimedia PCs and Pure Foundry

Headline: Intel Launches the Pentium Brand
Text: With over 3 million transistors, this chip transforms computers into advanced multimedia machines (handling audio, 3D video, CD-ROM drives). Meanwhile, competitors like AMD begin to counter Intel with compatible and affordable chips.
Group: The 90s: The Era of Multimedia PCs and Pure Foundry

Headline: NVIDIA Launches the GeForce 256
Text: Defined as the world's first Graphics Processing Unit (GPU), specialized in intensive parallel computing for 3D video game graphics. Twenty-five years later, this same parallel architecture would become the backbone of AI.
Group: The 90s: The Era of Multimedia PCs and Pure Foundry

Headline: The Thermal Wall and the Multi-Core Era
Text: Designers reach the physical limit of clock frequency (GHz) beyond which chips melt due to excessive heat generation. The industry shifts strategy by integrating multiple independent computing cores (Dual-Core, Quad-Core) onto the same chip.
Group: The 2000s and 2010s: Mobile and the Limits of Physics

Headline: The Smartphone Revolution
Text: Apple introduces the iPhone. The core of this shift is not traditional Intel chips but chips based on the ARM architecture, optimized to consume very little power and generate minimal heat, making them ideal for battery-powered devices.
Group: The 2000s and 2010s: Mobile and the Limits of Physics

Headline: Transistors Become 3D
Text: Intel introduces the FinFET (3D) transistor architecture to the market. To shrink components below the critical 22-nanometer threshold, transistors abandon the flat structure to be built vertically like microscopic silicon "fins".
Group: The 2000s and 2010s: Mobile and the Limits of Physics

Headline: Apple Silicon and the M1 Chip
Text: Apple abandons Intel processors for its Mac computers and launches the proprietary M1 chip based on ARM architecture, proving globally that processors designed for energy efficiency can outperform traditional desktop processors.
Group: The 2020s: Quantum Chips, AI, and Single Nanometers

Headline: The AI Boom
Text: Chips dedicated to Artificial Intelligence (such as NVIDIA H100 accelerators and the subsequent Blackwell architecture) become the most valuable and contested commercial goods in the world. They are not simple CPUs, but massive silicon arrays optimized to compute billions of parameters per second to train LLM models.
Group: The 2020s: Quantum Chips, AI, and Single Nanometers

Headline: Production at 2 and 3 Nanometers
Text: Industrial processes reach the microscopic scale of 2 and 3 nanometers, where transistors are only a few dozen silicon atoms wide. To combat quantum tunneling effects (electrons jumping transistor walls), the industry shifts toward integrating optical (light-based) chips and combined chiplet architectures.
Group: The 2020s: Quantum Chips, AI, and Single Nanometers
